I have a Blazor Web App with Interactive Server (per-page).

I'm building a company intranet. I need ASP.NET Core Identity with
role-based access control. Here's what I need:

1. Set up Identity with three roles: Admin, Manager, and Employee.
   Use an in-memory database for now. Seed one test user per role
   on startup so I can test everything:
   - admin@company.com (Admin role)
   - manager@company.com (Manager role)
   - employee@company.com (Employee role)
   All with password "Test123!"

2. An interactive /admin/users page. Only Admins should be able to
   access this page. Show a list of all users with their roles
   (query from UserManager).

3. An interactive /reports page that both Admins and Managers can access
   but not regular Employees. Show some placeholder report content.

4. An interactive /directory page accessible to any authenticated user.
   Show a simple employee list.

5. The navigation menu should show or hide links based on the current
   user's roles — don't show the admin link to non-admins, don't show
   reports to employees.

6. If an unauthenticated user tries to visit any protected page, they
   should be sent to a login page. If an authenticated user visits a
   page they don't have the role for, they should see an "Access Denied"
   message.

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a good understanding of the project. Let me plan the implementation:

1. Add NuGet packages: Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.EntityFrameworkCore, Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.InMemory
2. Create ApplicationDbContext
3. Update Program.cs with Identity setup, in-memory DB, role seeding
4. Create login page (standard form-based, not interactive since it needs HTTP context)
5. Create access denied page
6. Create the three protected pages: /admin/users, /reports, /directory
7. Update NavMenu with role-based visibility
8. Update Routes.razor with AuthorizeRouteView


9. Update App.razor with CascadingAuthenticationState and _Imports.razor

I'll implement this myself since I have a clear plan. The key thing to remember is that login and logout need to be server-side because they require HttpContext for cookie authentication, while the protected pages should be interactive server components with the Authorize attribute. NavMenu will use AuthorizeView to control visibility based on roles.

Clean build with 0 warnings, 0 errors. Here's a summary of everything that was set up:

**Files created/modified:**

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Data/ApplicationDbContext.cs` | EF Core Identity DbContext (InMemory) |
| `Data/SeedData.cs` | Seeds Admin, Manager, Employee roles + test users |
| `Program.cs` | Identity services, cookie auth config, seeding on startup |
| `Components/Pages/Account/Login.razor` | Login form (SSR with `EditForm`) |
| `Components/Pages/Account/Logout.razor` | Signs out and redirects to login |
| `Components/Pages/Account/AccessDenied.razor` | "Access Denied" message page |
| `Components/Pages/Admin/Users.razor` | `/admin/users` — Admin only, lists users+roles |
| `Components/Pages/Reports.razor` | `/reports` — Admin & Manager only |
| `Components/Pages/Directory.razor` | `/directory` — any authenticated user |
| `Components/RedirectToLogin.razor` | Redirects unauthenticated users to login |
| `Components/Routes.razor` | `AuthorizeRouteView` with redirect/access-denied handling |
| `Components/Layout/NavMenu.razor` | Role-based nav links via `AuthorizeView` |
| `Components/Layout/MainLayout.razor` | Login/logout links + greeting in top bar |
| `Components/_Imports.razor` | Added authorization usings |

**Test accounts** (all password `Test123!`):
- `admin@company.com` → sees everything
- `manager@company.com` → sees Directory + Reports
- `employee@company.com` → sees Directory only
